mainmatter.p: It follows from our characterization of positivity in terms of partial elements that an element a: A of a pointed dcpo A is positive if and only if it is strictly above the bottom element, i.e. \bot \sqsubset a.
(See also Proposition 6.2.12 of Tom de Jong’s thesis.)

So far we have studied positivity as an order-theoretic property of domains; in this section, we explore a non-order-theoretic generalization of this condition that is applicable in the setting of axiomatic domain theory. This notion is inspired by the alternative characterization positivity in terms of partial elements.

frontmatter.title: The Sierpiński interval of a dominion

mainmatter.p: Let \mathcal{C} be a category with pullbacks, a terminal object and let \mathcal{M} be a dominion on \mathcal{C}. A Sierpiński space for \mathcal{M} is defined to be a universal \mathcal{M}-partial map, i.e. a morphism { \mathbf{1} _{ \mathcal{C} } } \xrightarrow{{ \top }} { \Sigma } \in \mathcal{M} such that for any { U } \xrightarrow{{ i }} { A } \in \mathcal{M} there exists a unique map { A } \xrightarrow{{ \chi _i }} { \Sigma } making the following square cartesian:

When \mathcal{C} has an initial object \mathbf{0} _{ \mathcal{C} } and every { \mathbf{0} _{ \mathcal{C} } } \xrightarrow{{ }} { A } lies in \mathcal{M}, the Sierpiński space becomes an “interval object” { \left ( \Sigma , \top , \bot \right )} where { \mathbf{1} _{ \mathcal{C} } } \xrightarrow{{ \bot }} { \Sigma } is given as follows:
Under these circumstances, we refer to \mathcal{M} as a dominion that has a Sierpiński interval.

Let \mathcal{C} be category with pullbacks, a terminal object, and an initial object, equipped with a dominion \mathcal{M} that has a Sierpiński interval { \left ( \Sigma , \top , \bot \right )}. A path from { I } \xrightarrow{{ f }} { C } to { I } \xrightarrow{{ g }} { C } in \mathcal{C} is defined to be a morphism { \Sigma \times I } \xrightarrow{{ p }} { C } such that the following diagram commutes:
We shall write p:f \leadsto g to denote a path from f to g.

Let \mathcal{C} be category with pullbacks, a terminal object, and an initial object, equipped with a dominion \mathcal{M} that has a Sierpiński interval { \left ( \Sigma , \top , \bot \right )}, such that \mathcal{M}-partial map classifiers LA exist for all A \in \mathcal{C}. We will write \mathbb{L} = { \left ( L, \eta , \mu \right )} for the resulting partial map classifier monad on \mathcal{C}.

Let { \left ( A, \alpha \right )} be an \mathbb{L}-algebra in \mathcal{C}, and let { C } \xrightarrow{{ x }} { A } be a generalized element of A. We say that { C } \xrightarrow{{ x }} { A } is positive when for any { C } \xrightarrow{{ u }} { LA } such that there exists a path from { C } \xrightarrow{{ x }} { A } to { C } \xrightarrow{{ u; \alpha }} { A }, then { C } \xrightarrow{{ u }} { LA } factors through some (necessarily unique) { C } \xrightarrow{{ y }} { A } as below:

frontmatter.title: The two notions of positivity coincide for pointed dcpos

mainmatter.p: Let A be a pointed dcpo. Then an element x:A is a positive element of the pointed dcpo if and only if { \mathbf{1} _{ } } \xrightarrow{{ x }} { A } is a positive element in the dominion \mathcal{O} of Scott-open immersions of dcpos.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We first observe that the latter condition is equivalent to the following:
block.blockquote: For any u \in LA, if a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: u{ \downarrow }}u[p] then u{ \downarrow }= \top.
block.p: This follows from the (constructive) characterization of the path order in dcpos. Therefore, our result follows from our earlier characterization of positive elements of a pointed dcpo in terms of partial elements.

frontmatter.title: Positive generation and open positivity predicates

mainmatter.p: In a lifted dcpo \mathsf{L} {A}, the positive elements form an open subdcpo { \left ( \mathsf{L} {A} \right )} ^+ \cong A: thus, it follows that positivity is always an open property in classical mathematics. On the other hand, it seems that constructive mathematics does not necessarily assert that the positive elements form an open subspace in general. In this section, we grapple with this subtlety by studying the relationship between openness of A^+ and a desirable property that we shall refer to as positive generation, which states that every element of A is the supremum of its “positive part”.
We say that a pointed dcpo A has an open positivity predicate if and only if any of the following equivalent conditions hold:

ol.li: The positivity predicate { \Gamma A } \xrightarrow{{ \mathsf{pos} }} { \Omega } tracks a continuous morphism of dcpos { A } \xrightarrow{{ \mathsf{pos} }} { \Sigma }.
The embedding { A^+ } \xrightarrow{{ \iota ^+ }} { A } is a Scott-open immersion.
For a directed subset I \subseteq A, if \bigsqcup{A} is positive then there exists an element a \in I that is positive in A.

mainmatter.p: Let A be a dcpo; then an element a:A is called positively generated when it is the supremum of the semidirected subset { \left \{ b:A \mid b=a \land \mathsf{pos} \,{a} \right \} }, i.e. we have a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf{pos} \, a}a.
An element a of a pointed dcpo A is called non-negatively generated when a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{ \lnot { \left ( a= \bot \right )} }a holds.

frontmatter.title: Positively generated elements are non-negatively generated

mainmatter.p: Let a be a positively generated element of a pointed dcpo A. Then a is also non-negatively generated.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We must check that a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \lnot { \left ( a = \bot \right )} }a. By assumption, it suffices to check that \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf{pos} \, a}a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \lnot { \left ( a= \bot \right )} }a, which follows because the bottom element is not positive.

frontmatter.title: Bottom and non-bottom elements are non-negatively generated

mainmatter.p: In a pointed dcpo, any non-bottom element is non-negatively generated.

ol.li: The bottom element \bot.
Any compact element a:A for which \lnot { \left ( a= \bot \right )} holds.

mainmatter.p: Hence, any element a for which it is decidable whether a= \bot is non-negatively generated.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: The bottom element is clearly non-negatively generated, as it lies beneath any element. Now fix a:A and assume \lnot { \left ( a= \bot \right )}; we must check that a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \lnot { \left ( a= \bot \right )} }a but the latter is equal to a.

mainmatter.p: When a pointed dcpo A satisfies either of the following equivalent conditions, we will refer to it as positively generated.

ol.li: Every element a: A is positively generated, i.e. we have a = \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf{pos} \, a}a.
For any semidirected subset I \subseteq A, we have \bigsqcup I = \bigsqcup { \left \{ a \in I \mid \mathsf{pos} \, a \right \} }.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: (2) implies (1) by setting I = { \left \{ a \right \} }. Conversely, assume that each element is the supremum of its positive part and fix a semidirected subset I \subseteq A. We compute:
block.tex: \bigsqcup { \left \{ a \in I \mid \mathsf{pos} \, a \right \} } = \bigsqcup _{a \in { \left \{ a \in I \mid \mathsf{pos} \, a \right \} } }a = \bigsqcup _{a \in I} \bigsqcup _{p \in \mathsf{pos} \, a} a = \bigsqcup _{a \in I} a

frontmatter.title: Positively generated dcpos have open positivity predicates

mainmatter.p: Let A be a positively generated pointed dcpo. Then A has an open positivity predicate.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Let I \subseteq A be a directed subset such that \bigsqcup{I} is positive; we must find a positive element of I. Because A is positively generated we know that \bigsqcup{I} \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup { \left ( I \cap A^+ \right )}. Because \bigsqcup{I} is positive and I \cap A^+ is semidirected, it follows that the latter is inhabited.

mainmatter.p: Let A be a dcpo; then the lift \mathsf{L} {A} is positively generated.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Let u= { \left ( \phi ,a \right )} be an element of \mathsf{L} {A}. Note that u is equal to \bigsqcup _{p: \phi }u; as definedness coincides with positivity in lifted dcpos, we have \phi = \mathsf{pos} \, u, and so we are done.

frontmatter.title: Lifted dcpos have open positivity predicates

mainmatter.p: Let A be a dcpo; then the lifting \mathsf{L} {A} has an open positivity predicate.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: It suffices to recall that a lifted dcpo is positively generated.

frontmatter.title: When is a continuous pointed dcpo positively generated?

mainmatter.p: We describe some light conditions under which a continuous dcpo is positively generated.

frontmatter.title: Non-negatively generated basis yields positive generation

mainmatter.p: Let A be a pointed continuous dcpo presented by a basis B \subseteq A such that each element of B is non-negatively generated in A. Then A is positively generated.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We must show that for any a \in A, we have a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf{pos} \, a}a. Let B_a be the subset { \left \{ b \in B \mid b \ll a \right \} }. By assumption, it suffices to check that \bigsqcup B_a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf{pos} \, a}a. Fixing b \in B_a, we must check that b \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf{pos} \, a}a.

As b is assumed to be non-negatively generated, we have b= \bigsqcup _{p: \lnot { \left ( b= \bot \right )} }b so to check b \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf{pos} \, a}a it suffices to check that \lnot { \left ( b= \bot \right )} implies that a is positive. We fix semidirected J \subseteq A such that a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup{J} = \bigsqcup { \left ( { \left \{ \bot \right \} } \cup J \right )} to check that J is inhabited. We recall that b \ll a and a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup { \left ( { \left \{ \bot \right \} } \cup J \right )}, so it follows by the definition of the way-below relation that either b \sqsubseteq \bot or there exists j \in J such that b \sqsubseteq j. The first case is contradictory by our assumption that \lnot { \left ( b= \bot \right )}, and the second case verifies that J is inhabited, so a is positive.

frontmatter.title: Basis with decidable bottomhood yields positive generation

mainmatter.p: Let A be a pointed continuous dcpo presented by a basis B \subseteq A for each element b \in B it is decidable whether b= \bot. Then A is positively generated.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We observe that every element of B is non-negatively generated, so we may conclude that A is positively generated.

frontmatter.title: Positively generated basis yields positive generation

mainmatter.p: Let A be a pointed continuous dcpo presented by a basis B \subseteq A whose elements are all positively generated in A. Then A is positively generated.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: As positive generation implies non-negative generation for an element of a pointed dcpo, we may conclude that A is positively generated.

mainmatter.p: I am not sure how useful the following construction is. On the other hand, maybe it has a universal property at the level of posets.

frontmatter.title: The positively generated core of a dcpo

mainmatter.p: Let A be a pointed dcpo; the subposet A ^{ \mathit{pg} } \subseteq{A} spanned by positively generated elements is closed under directed suprema, and thus gives rise to a subdcpo that we shall refer to as the positively generated core of A.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Let I \subseteq A be a directed subset whose elements are all positively generated. We must check that the supremum \bigsqcup{I} is positively generated, i.e. \bigsqcup{I} \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf{pos} \, \bigsqcup{I} } \bigsqcup{I}. Fixing i \in I, we must check that i \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf{pos} \,{\bigsqcup{I}} } \bigsqcup{I}. As i is positively generated, we have i \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf{pos} \,{i} }i. As positivity is upward-closed, we have \mathsf{pos} \,{i} \sqsubseteq \mathsf{pos} \, \bigsqcup{I}. Therefore, we deduce i \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf{pos} \, i}i \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf{pos} \,{\bigsqcup{I}} } \bigsqcup{I}.

frontmatter.title: Characterizing freeness in terms of positivity

mainmatter.p: In classical mathematics, every \mathbb{L}-algebra is free; this does not appear to be asserted by constructive mathematics, but in this section we verify that freeness can be characterized in terms of positivity. The main result is that a pointed dcpo A is positively generated if and only the evident map { \mathsf{L} {A^+} } \xrightarrow{{ }} { A } is an isomorphism.

Let A be a pointed dcpo with an open positivity predicate; we define the “purification” map { A } \xrightarrow{{ \mathsf{purify} _A }} { \mathsf{L} {A^+} } into the lift of the subdcpo of positive elements to send a \in A to the partial element { \left ( \mathsf{pos} \,{a} , \lambda p. a \right )}; we note:
The mapping defined above is continuous because we have assumed that A has an open positivity predicate, so its domain of definition defines a Scott-open subdcpo of A.

frontmatter.title: Pointed dcpos with open positivity predicates are almost free

mainmatter.p: Let A be a pointed dcpo with an open positivity predicate; then the purification map { A } \xrightarrow{{ \mathsf{purify} _A }} { \mathsf{L} {A^+} } is the projection part of an embedding-projection pair { \left ( \mathsf{L} { \iota ^+}; \alpha _A \right )} \dashv \mathsf{purify} _A.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We fix { \left ( \phi ,a^+ \right )} : \mathsf{L} {A^+} and compute explicitly using the fact that \mathsf{purify} _A preserves the bottom element and therefore preserves semidirected subsets:

For the deflation, we fix a:A and observe that \alpha _A { \left ( \mathsf{L} { \iota ^+} { \left ( \mathsf{purify} _A a \right )} \right )} = \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf{pos} \, a} a which is evidently below a.

frontmatter.title: Positively generated pointed dcpos are free

mainmatter.p: Let A be a positively generated pointed dcpo; then A is canonically free on its subdcpo of positive elements A^+. More specifically, the embedding-projection pair { \left ( \mathsf{L} { \iota ^+}; \alpha _A \right )} \dashv { A } \xrightarrow{{ \mathsf{purify} _A }} { \mathsf{L} {A^+} } given by the purification map is an isomorphism of dcpos.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: First, we recall that the embedding-projection pair { \left ( \mathsf{L} { \iota ^+}; \alpha _A \right )} \dashv \mathsf{purify} _A exists because A has an open positivity predicate by virtue of its positive generation. Therefore, the statement of this lemma is well-formed.

Because { \left ( \mathsf{L} { \iota ^+}; \alpha _A \right )} \dashv \mathsf{purify} _A is an embedding-projection pair, it remains only to check that \mathsf{purify} _A; \mathsf{L} { \iota ^+}; \alpha _A is an inflation, i.e. for all a:A we must have a \sqsubseteq \alpha _A { \left ( \mathsf{L} { \iota ^+} { \left ( \mathsf{purify} _Aa \right )} \right )}. As the latter is equal to \bigsqcup _{p: \mathsf{pos} \, a}a, the inflation condition follows from our assumption that A is positively generated.

mainmatter.p: A pointed dcpo A is lifted if and only if it is positively generated.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: This follows from two existing lemmas:

ol.li: Lifted dcpos (free algebras) are positively generated.
Positively generated pointed dcpos are free.

blockquote.p: I am Professor of Computer Security and Head of the Department of Computer Science and Technology, often informally called The Computer Laboratory. I am also the Robin Walker Fellow in Computer Science at Queens' College, Cambridge.

My research work examines the security and privacy of large-scale distributed computer systems. Within this broad area, I am currently interested in the security and privacy of networked mobile devices, such as smartphones, tablets and laptops. I examine the security of the devices themselves as well as the security and privacy problems induced by the interaction between mobile devices and cloud-based Internet services. I approach this through the critical evaluation of existing products, by designing and building novel prototype technologies, and by measuring human behaviour.

mainmatter.blockquote: I study programming languages, type theories, and logics. I am particularly interested in applying semantic methods to prove syntactic properties of modal type theories and programming languages. I am also involved in the development of program logics for concurrent programming languages through the Iris project.

mainmatter.p: Villum Investigator; Head of Logic and Semantics Group.

Had a very interesting chat with Nathan Corbyn and Marcelo Fiore this afternoon. Nathan Corbyn described his work on normalization-by-evaluation in gluing style, which he has formalized computationally in Agda via setoids. Nathan Corbyn aims to capture TDPE in a principled way, including the Futamura projections(!); Marcelo Fiore is suggesting that we start from the free extension of \lambda-calculus by a model of a given algebraic theory (which I think we basically understand), and try to move from this to handling second-order algebraic theories. We will come back to this tomorrow.

frontmatter.title: Reflections on existential types
frontmatter.meta: @article{sterling-2022-existentials, doi = {10.48550/ARXIV.2210.00758}, author = {Sterling, Jonathan}, title = {Reflections on existential types}, publisher = {arXiv}, year = {2022}, note = {Unpublished manuscript}, }

mainmatter.p: Existential types are reconstructed in terms of small reflective subuniverses and dependent sums. The folklore decomposition detailed here gives rise to a particularly simple account of first class modules as a mode of use of traditional second class modules in connection with the modal operator induced by a reflective subuniverse, leading to a semantic justification for the rules of first-class modules in languages like OCaml and MoscowML. Additionally, we expose several constructions that give rise to semantic models of ML-style programming languages with both first-class modules and realistic computational effects, culminating in a model that accommodates higher-order first class recursive modules and higher-order store.

frontmatter.title: Presenting dcpos and dcpo algebras
frontmatter.meta: @article{jung-moshier-vickers-2008, author = {Achim Jung and M. Andrew Moshier and Steve Vickers}, doi = {10.1016/j.entcs.2008.10.013}, issn = {1571-0661}, journal = {Electronic Notes in Theoretical Computer Science}, keywords = {Dcpo algebras, dcpo presentations, d-topology, -topology, coverage theorems}, note = {Proceedings of the 24th Conference on the Mathematical Foundations of Programming Semantics (MFPS XXIV)}, pages = {209-229}, title = {Presenting Dcpos and Dcpo Algebras}, volume = {218}, year = {2008}, }

mainmatter.p: Dcpos can be presented by preorders of generators and inequational relations expressed as covers. Algebraic operations on the generators (possibly with their results being ideals of generators) can be extended to the dcpo presented, provided the covers are “stable” for the operations. The resulting dcpo algebra has a natural universal characterization and satisfies all the inequational laws satisfied by the generating algebra. Applications include known “coverage theorems” from locale theory.
